turns.text: Hello Elenore, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s strange to be on this side of the desk, honestly. Usually, I’m the one looking at the scans, checking the pixels for the right markers, but now… now I’m the one trying to get pregnant. I’m eighteen, I live in Peabody, and I know exactly how complex it is just to get a healthy fetus through the first trimester. Most girls my age are worried about prom or finals, but I’m here tracking my cycle and silently calculating the risks of neural tube defects. 

I’m terrified. Not of the birth, but of that silence in the ultrasound room. I know what a heart rate looks like when it’s struggling, and I know what that "oh" sounds like when a technician finds something off. I don't want to be the patient who identifies a problem before the doctor even realizes what they’re looking at. 

I just need to find a provider who doesn't see "eighteen" and "never married" and immediately hand me a pamphlet with cartoons on it. I don’t need the simplified version; I need someone who won't get annoyed when I ask for the data. I want to be a mom, but I’m having a hell of a time turning off the clinical researcher in my brain. It’s hard to trust the process when you know exactly how many things can break.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Honestly, I think I expected a partnership. In my head, I envisioned sitting down with an OB-GYN who would look at my charts, see that I understand the mechanics of early development, and meet me on that level. I expected a real conversation about preconception screenings, maybe talking through the limitations of current diagnostic technology. I wanted a peer-to-peer discussion where I could voice my anxieties about trophoblast differentiation and have them be validated, not dismissed as "typical first-time mom nerves."

The reality has been… patronizing. When I walk into a clinic, they see an eighteen-year-old girl and they shift into this weird, gentle, almost condescending tone. They want to talk about prenatal vitamins and the importance of sleep, which, fine, I know that. But when I try to steer the conversation toward more technical specifics—like the sensitivity of NIPTs or when to schedule the early morphology scans to maximize diagnostic yield—they just smile and tell me to "relax" and "let nature take its course."

It makes me feel so small. I’m not a child, and I’m definitely not ignorant. It’s infuriating to be treated like I’m asking for something unreasonable just because I want to know exactly what’s happening inside my body. It feels like they’re gatekeeping the information, and that just makes me trust the process even less.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest shock was definitely the wall of dismissal. I went into that first appointment prepared to discuss my health with the precision of a colleague, but the provider didn't even look at me as a person with agency. She spent the first ten minutes asking about my home life and my plans for school, almost like she was auditing my suitability to be a mother rather than discussing my physiological health. 

When I finally steered the conversation toward preconception screening and my concerns about potential markers, she literally chuckled. She called me "precocious." That one word felt like a slap. It stripped away all the work I’ve put into understanding my own body and reduced it to a cute quirk of a teenager who’s reading too much online. 

It was a negative surprise, but it was clarifying. I realized that my clinical background is actually a liability in their eyes because it makes me "difficult." They want a patient who says "yes" and "okay" and goes home to paint a nursery. They don’t want a patient who knows the window for neural tube closure or who asks about the specific detection rates of the screenings they’re ordering. I left that office feeling more isolated than when I walked in, realizing I’m going to have to fight for the kind of transparency I deserve.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I thought I’d feel like a scientist entering a controlled experiment. I expected to feel empowered, really. I’ve spent so long studying fetal development in the abstract that I thought my knowledge would act like an armor. I figured I’d be the woman who stayed calm during the early stages because I’d understand the "why" behind every symptom and every milestone. I thought I’d be able to separate the clinical data from the human experience—that I’d manage the anxiety with logic.

The reality? It’s completely dismantled that. I didn’t account for the visceral, terrifying feeling of *hope* overriding my ability to compartmentalize. It’s impossible to be the scientist when you’re the one hoping for the outcome. Every time I get a cramp, I’m not just "observing physiological changes"—I’m spiraling into a thousand worst-case scenarios I’ve seen on a monitor. 

The weight of knowing too much is actually heavier than I imagined. It turns out, I can’t "logic" my way out of the fear that something will go wrong. I’m not just waiting for a biological process; I’m waiting for a miracle that I feel I’ve already deconstructed into pieces. It’s exhausting to know how many cells have to divide perfectly, and it makes the "miracle" part feel like a terrifying gamble.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

The biggest void is definitely the lack of intellectual space. From the professionals, I’m missing a provider who sees my medical literacy as an asset rather than a symptom of "anxiety." I don’t need to be coddled; I need to be taken seriously. I need a provider who can say, "Elenore, I understand your concern about this specific statistic, so let's look at the data together so you can breathe." Instead, I get forced into this "patient" box that feels like a cage.

In my wider network, it’s even harder. My peers are entirely absent from this reality; they look at me like I’m from another planet because I’m prioritizing fetal growth measurements over my social life. And because I’m not married and I’m so young, the adults in my life tend to treat this as a "mistake" or a "phase" rather than a deeply intentional, albeit terrifying, life choice. 

There’s no one to hold that dual space for me—the space where I’m a young, first-time mom who is genuinely scared, and simultaneously the person who understands the risks better than most. I feel like I’m carrying the weight of the "clinical researcher" in a world that only wants to see a "teenage girl." It’s a lonely place to be.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I expected to find a middle ground—something that bridged the gap between basic "Baby Center" style forums and actual peer-reviewed journals. I wanted apps that would offer me more than just a countdown to ovulation or tips on what foods to avoid. I was looking for tools that provided data-backed insights, maybe some interactive models of what’s happening at a cellular level, or at least a place to track specific, granular biometrics.

What I actually found was… incredibly frustrating. Most of these apps feel like they’re designed for a five-year-old. They’re all soft pastels, overly cheerful emojis, and articles that start with "Congratulations, Mama!" and offer absolutely zero substance. They use such patronizing, simplified language that it honestly insults my intelligence. They tell me to "trust my body" and "focus on my breath" instead of giving me the statistics or the biological clarity I’m craving.

It’s like the entire industry assumes that if you’re trying to conceive, your brain cells just vanish. There is no space for a user who wants to track the probability of implantation based on specific hormonal fluctuations or someone who understands the morphology of early development. I find myself clicking "ignore" on all the pop-up advice, desperately scrolling for actual technical information that doesn’t exist in these spaces. I’m drowning in "fluff" when all I want is the truth.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The biggest gap is in the *tone* and the *scope* of the information. The apps and websites tell me I’m in charge—they frame it as an empowering journey where I’m "tracking my miracle." They make it sound like if I just follow their color-coded calendar and eat the right vitamins, everything is guaranteed to go perfectly. It feels like a fantasy world where nothing ever goes wrong, which, frankly, makes me even more anxious because I know better.

Then I go to the doctor, and the reality is the exact opposite. They don’t treat me like an empowered participant; they treat me like a passive recipient of their "wisdom." If the apps are selling me a dream, the clinic is trying to keep me in the dark. 

The most jarring gap is the lack of *risk transparency*. The apps never talk about the "heavy silence" I’m so afraid of—they only show pictures of smiling babies. And when I ask my doctor for more detailed stats on miscarriage or developmental markers, they treat me like I’m being morbid or difficult. So, I’m stuck between digital sources that want me to be a bubbly, carefree girl, and providers who want me to be a quiet, compliant patient. Neither one acknowledges the person I actually am: someone who knows the stakes and is terrified, but still determined.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

Even though I don't have a chronic illness in the traditional sense, I do have a pre-existing "condition"—my own background in medical imaging. I expected the healthcare system to see that as a *tool* to help me prepare. I walked in expecting them to acknowledge that because I know the mechanics of fetal development, they could be more transparent with me, not less. I expected a collaborative, high-level discussion where we could map out a plan for my prenatal care that respected my knowledge of anatomy and imaging.

Instead, the moment I opened my mouth to discuss the intricacies of fetal development or my technical concerns, it was like they put up a screen. The "preparation" I received was generic; they gave me a standard packet of information for a first-time mother, as if I hadn't already read a dozen textbooks on the subject. 

They didn't want to collaborate; they wanted to manage me. It’s like they view my intelligence as a "complication" to be managed rather than a resource. I didn’t get a partner in care; I got a lecture on why I shouldn't be reading so much. It felt like they were trying to gaslight me into forgetting what I know, which just made me prepare by spending even more time in the literature, further isolating myself in the process.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

There wasn't a single moment of proactive support. Not one. Nobody reached out to ask about my concerns or to acknowledge the unique anxiety of someone who works in this field. If I hadn’t been the one to bring up specific questions or push for information, I would have just been another face in the waiting room receiving the "standard" low-risk care protocol. The system is built for passive patients, and it assumes that if you aren't asking, you don't need to know.

I’ve had to fight for every bit of acknowledgment. I have to consciously decide, before I walk into a room, how much of my professional vocabulary I’m going to use. Do I use the correct terms for specific organ structures and risk flagging, or do I "dumb it down" so they don't look at me like I'm a nuisance? It’s exhausting to perform this balancing act. I’m constantly advocating for the right to be treated like an intelligent adult, and it’s demoralizing. I shouldn’t have to "earn" a real conversation with my own doctor by proving I’ve done my homework, but that’s the reality I’m living in. The system doesn't meet you where you are; it forces you to fit into its pre-approved, simplistic mold.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

This is... a difficult topic to talk about. To be honest, I haven't really shared how much I'm struggling with this part of it. Before, I imagined a partnership that looked like a mirror image of my own intensity—someone who would want to dive into the technical details with me, someone who would be at my side during every appointment, asking the questions I might be too afraid to voice. I wanted a teammate who would help carry the mental load of all these "what-ifs."

The reality is much quieter, and it hurts. He is supportive in a very traditional, superficial way—he buys the supplements, he keeps the house calm, he tells me "everything will be fine" like it’s a mantra. But he doesn't want to hear about the statistics of chromosomal abnormalities or the nuances of early organ development. To him, those conversations are just me "worrying too much" and ruining the excitement of it all.

It’s created this wall between us. I’m the only one sitting in the middle of the night, spiraling over the probability of specific developmental outcomes, and when I try to bring him into that world, he just shuts down. It makes me feel even more isolated. I’m the one carrying the knowledge, the fear, and the clinical awareness, and it feels like I’m doing the entire preparation process alone. I didn't expect to feel this lonely in a relationship where we're supposed to be building a family together.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

It’s not just "moments"—it’s been the entire foundation of this journey. The burden is completely lopsided. What drives it is the intersection of my professional background and the social expectations placed on women. Because I have the clinical vocabulary, I feel a self-imposed duty to "monitor" things, but because I’m eighteen and unmarried, the medical system and even my partner seem to relegate me to the role of the "emotional, anxious teenager."

My partner feels that by being "relaxed," he is providing balance, but to me, it just feels like negligence. He gets to stay in this state of blissful ignorance because he knows *I* am keeping track of the appointments, the genetic testing windows, and the research. I’m the one who reads the white papers on prenatal diagnostic sensitivities so that I don't get blindsided in the ultrasound room. 

I’m carrying the clinical weight *and* the emotional weight. I feel like I’m the only one in the room who truly understands the gravity of what we’re embarking on. If I stop researching, I feel like the safety net vanishes. It’s an exhausting, heavy kind of advocacy that I never asked for, but I’m terrified that if I let go, I’m failing the baby before they’re even conceived. It’s not just a partnership; it’s me performing labor while everyone else watches me "worry."
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't experienced a loss, not yet. But honestly, that’s exactly what haunts me. I’ve seen the way the system treats people who have; they get slotted into these clinical pathways that are all about "risk management," which is basically just checking boxes on a form. My fear isn't just the loss itself—it’s the prospect of being treated like a statistic if it happens. 

I know that if I were to miscarry, I wouldn't get the space to process it as a person. I would just be a "case of early pregnancy loss" to the clinic, something to be managed and filed away so they can move on to the next patient in the waiting room. That’s what I see happen every day in the medical world. 

When I look at my own journey, I expect that if something goes wrong, they won't treat me with any more nuance than they do now. They’ll just give me the "I'm sorry, it happens" line and tell me to try again in a few months. There’s no infrastructure for the kind of emotional, informed, and patient-centered care that someone like me actually needs. It’s all about the numbers and the protocol, and that makes the prospect of a future loss feel even more lonely. You’re just a file, not a mother-to-be.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

I think there might be a misunderstanding—I haven't actually experienced a pregnancy loss yet. But if I’m being honest, that fear is a huge part of why I’m so rigid about the clinical details right now. Even without the history of loss, I feel like I'm already mourning the possibility of it every single day. 

I see women come into the clinic after a loss, and the staff is so quick to tell them, "You're young, you’ll try again," or "It’s common, don’t stress." That complete invalidation of their grief—the way it gets reduced to a biological hiccup—is exactly what I’m terrified of. I know too much about the biological reality of *why* those things happen, so for a provider to dismiss a loss so casually, it just feels like they aren't acknowledging the human life that was there.

The fact that I haven't lost a pregnancy doesn't make my fear any less real. In some ways, it feels like I'm trying to prepare for an inevitable, invisible cliff. When I talk about my anxieties, even now, the providers treat me like I’m inventing problems. If they dismiss me now, when I'm "low risk," I know they wouldn't offer me a shred of genuine empathy if I actually *did* lose a pregnancy. It makes me feel like I’m holding my breath, waiting for a reality check that I’m not even sure I’m strong enough to handle.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

There is one thing. I want people to understand that being "informed" isn’t the same as being "anxious," even though providers constantly conflate the two. 

When I walk into a clinic and start asking about the nuances of ultrasound resolution or the predictive value of certain markers, I’m not doing it because I’m a neurotic teenager. I’m doing it because I’m trying to regain some sense of agency in a process that feels inherently out of control. My clinical background is the only thing that makes me feel like I’m not just a passenger in my own body. 

But it’s isolating. I feel like I’m standing behind a wall of glass; I can see the medical system from the inside out, and it’s cold. It lacks the humanity it claims to protect. If someone could just say, "I see you, I see your intelligence, and I’m going to use it to help you navigate this," I think I could finally exhale. 

I’m tired of being treated like an outlier because I want to be an active participant. I’m just an eighteen-year-old girl who wants the same thing everyone else does: a healthy baby and a provider who looks me in the eye and treats me like an adult. Is that really so much to ask?
